children.name: Accipiter
children.description: Goshawks and sparrowhawks — the archetypal bird-hunting hawks, with short rounded wings and long tails adapted for rapid manoeuvre through woodland. The Northern Goshawk is the apex forest raptor of the temperate Northern Hemisphere, capable of overtaking prey at speed through dense trees. The Eurasian Sparrowhawk is the most numerous hawk in Britain and Europe, ambushing small birds at garden feeders and woodland edges in an explosive burst of acceleration. Both species show strong reverse sexual dimorphism — females are dramatically larger than males.

children.name: Accipiter gentilis
children.description: The 'cook's hawk' of medieval falconry, valued above all others for hunting hares and pheasants. Once extirpated from Britain by gamekeepers; now recolonising from escaped falconry birds and deliberate reintroductions.

children.name: Accipiter nisus
children.description: The most abundant raptor in Europe. DDT-related eggshell thinning collapsed populations post-WWII; recovery after the pesticide ban was one of the first demonstrations of successful raptor conservation.

children.name: Accipiter striatus
children.description: The smallest hawk in North America, a dashing bird-hunter of forests and a familiar visitor to backyard feeders where it ambushes small birds.

children.name: Accipiter badius
children.description: A small, widespread sparrowhawk of Africa and southern Asia, a bold hunter of lizards, small birds and insects in open woodland and gardens.

children.name: Accipiter melanoleucus
children.description: The largest African sparrowhawk, a powerful forest hawk that preys heavily on doves, pigeons, and other medium-sized birds. Occurs in two morphs — the white morph has white underparts, the dark morph is almost entirely black — and is widespread from West Africa to South Africa in moist forest and well-wooded suburbs.

children.name: Accipiter superciliosus
children.description: The smallest member of the genus, barely larger than a starling, inhabiting humid lowland and foothill forest from Nicaragua to Bolivia. Despite its diminutive size it is an effective predator of hummingbirds and small passerines, which it chases through dense understorey vegetation at speed.

children.name: Accipiter tachiro
children.description: A compact, powerful goshawk of sub-Saharan forest and dense woodland, the male with blue-grey upperparts and rufous-barred underparts. An ambush predator of birds, lizards, and small mammals, it reveals its presence with its distinctive rapid display calls given from high perches in the canopy.

children.name: Accipiter rufiventris
children.description: A small montane sparrowhawk of forest edges and clearings in the highlands of eastern and southern Africa, recognised by the male's orange-rufous breast contrasting with blue-grey upperparts. An endemic African highland raptor found from Ethiopia south to the Cape.

children.name: Accipiter virgatus
children.description: A small forest sparrowhawk of South and Southeast Asian mountains from Pakistan to the Philippines, closely resembling the Eurasian Sparrowhawk but with more pronounced streaking on the underparts. It inhabits dense broadleaf and conifer forest at moderate to high elevations.

children.name: Accipiter albogularis
children.description: A distinctive pied hawk of Pacific island forests from the Solomons to Fiji, with contrasting black upperparts and white underparts. Found on islands where it may be the dominant avian forest predator, hunting birds, large insects, and lizards in the forest interior.

children.name: Accipiter fasciatus
children.description: The most widespread diurnal raptor in Australia and New Guinea, a versatile predator of birds, small mammals, and reptiles in almost every wooded habitat from rainforest to dry woodland. Highly adaptable, it has thrived even in fragmented suburban woodland.

children.name: Accipiter novaehollandiae
children.description: An Australian and New Guinean forest hawk that uniquely includes a fully white colour morph found nowhere else in the genus — an all-white bird with pink eyes set in a grey face. The white morph inhabits rainforest and dense coastal forest; the grey morph replaces it in dry woodland. Both hunt birds and other vertebrates by rapid ambush.

children.name: Buteo
children.description: Buzzards — broad-winged, soaring hawks of open and woodland landscapes. The Common Buzzard is the most abundant and widespread raptor in Europe, its mewing, cat-like call a characteristic sound of the upland and woodland edge. Buzzards are versatile and opportunistic hunters, taking rabbits, voles, earthworms, and carrion depending on season and availability. In North America, 'buzzard' colloquially refers to vultures, while what Europeans call buzzards are known as 'hawks' across the Atlantic.

children.name: Buteo buteo
children.description: The most numerous raptor in Britain. Plumage is highly variable from pale cream to near-black; carpal patches on the underside of the wing are a consistent ID feature. Declined massively due to persecution; recovered strongly since the 1970s.

children.name: Buteo lagopus
children.description: An Arctic-breeding buzzard that winters in open farmland across northern Europe. Distinguished by feathered legs (the source of the name: lagopus = 'hare-foot') and a distinctive black belly patch and white tail base.

children.name: Buteo jamaicensis
children.description: The most familiar and widespread raptor in North America, found from Alaska to Panama. Its scream is used as the default 'raptor sound' in virtually every film and television production, even when the bird shown is an eagle.

children.name: Buteo rufinus
children.description: A large pale buzzard of steppes and semi-deserts from southeastern Europe to Central Asia, often hunting rodents from a hovering position.

children.name: Buteo swainsoni
children.description: A slender prairie hawk that makes one of the longest migrations of any North American raptor, travelling in huge flocks to the pampas of Argentina.

children.name: Buteo lineatus
children.description: A boldly patterned hawk of moist lowland forest and riparian woodland in eastern North America and California, with rich rusty-orange barring on the underparts and a distinctive banded tail. A vocal species, its loud, repeated call is one of the characteristic sounds of eastern deciduous forest.

children.name: Buteo platypterus
children.description: A small, compact hawk that generates some of the largest raptor migration spectacles in the world, with hundreds of thousands streaming over Veracruz, Mexico, and Hawk Mountain, Pennsylvania, in autumn. In summer a bird of eastern deciduous forest, it winters from Mexico to Bolivia.

children.name: Buteo albonotatus
children.description: A slender, dark hawk of canyons and mountains from the southwestern US to South America that closely resembles the Turkey Vulture in flight — an apparent aggressive mimicry that may allow it to approach prey without triggering escape responses in vigilant animals habituated to vultures.

children.name: Buteo regalis
children.description: The largest North American hawk, a pale, long-winged bird of western grasslands and semi-desert, named for its rusty-reddish back and leg feathering. A specialist predator of prairie dogs and ground squirrels, it has declined with the loss of open shortgrass prairie and the suppression of prairie dog colonies.

children.name: Buteo auguralis
children.description: A small, distinctive buzzard of West and Central African forest edges and gallery forest, identifiable by its rufous tail and pale underparts. Largely resident in its humid zone, it hunts lizards, small mammals, and large insects.

children.name: Buteo trizonatus
children.description: A small buzzard endemic to the montane forests and forest edges of southern Africa from the Western Cape to Zimbabwe, replacing the Common Buzzard in highland habitats. It is compact and boldly patterned, a woodland specialist of the Afromontane belt.

children.name: Buteo hemilasius
children.description: The largest Asian Buteo, a powerful pale buzzard of the Tibetan Plateau, steppes, and open mountain landscapes of Central and East Asia. It is a dominant predator of pikas, ground squirrels, and other small mammals in treeless high-altitude habitats.

children.name: Buteo leucorrhous
children.description: A small, dark hawk of montane forest in the Andes from Venezuela to northwestern Argentina, immediately identified in flight by its contrasting white rump. One of the less frequently observed Andean raptors, it inhabits humid cloud forest and forest edges between 1,000 and 3,000 m.

children.name: Aquila
children.description: True eagles — large, powerful raptors of mountains, forests, and open plains. The Golden Eagle is the most widespread eagle in the Northern Hemisphere and one of the best-studied raptors in the world. It holds territories of up to 100 km² and is capable of taking prey up to the size of young red deer and mountain goats. Eagles have been symbols of power across human cultures for millennia — on Roman standards, medieval heraldry, and modern national emblems alike.

children.name: Aquila chrysaetos
children.description: Scotland holds the majority of the UK breeding population; English reintroduction is underway. In Scotland, a Golden Eagle territory in the Highlands can cover 50–80 km². The golden nape feathers are visible at close range and give the bird its name.

children.name: Aquila clanga
children.description: One of Europe's most endangered raptors; fewer than 1,000 pairs remain globally. Breeds in lowland riparian forest in eastern Europe and Russia. Hybridises extensively with Lesser Spotted Eagle where ranges overlap.

children.name: Aquila heliaca
children.description: Heraldic bird of the Habsburg Empire and still on the Czech coat of arms. A conservation success story: central European populations have recovered from near-extinction through nest protection and electrocution prevention on power pylons.

children.name: Aquila rapax
children.description: A widespread but declining eagle of African and South Asian savannas, an opportunistic predator and notorious pirate of other raptors' kills. Now Vulnerable.

children.name: Aquila nipalensis
children.description: A large migratory eagle of Central Asian steppes that winters in Africa and South Asia. Listed as Endangered owing to steep population declines.

children.name: Aquila verreauxii
children.description: A spectacular, jet-black eagle of rocky mountains and gorges in eastern and southern Africa, specialised almost exclusively on rock hyraxes (dassies) which may comprise 90% of its diet. Named after the French naturalist Jules Verreaux; it soars along cliff faces with masterly command and is one of the most sought-after eagles on the African continent.

children.name: Aquila gurneyi
children.description: A large, powerful forest eagle of New Guinea and the Maluku Islands, one of the largest raptors in Oceania. Named after the English ornithologist John Henry Gurney; it inhabits lowland and foothill forest where it hunts large birds, mammals, and reptiles from concealment.

children.name: Aquila audax
children.description: Australia's largest bird of prey, with a distinctive long, wedge-shaped tail and a wingspan approaching 2.3 m. Once persecuted as a supposed threat to livestock and with government bounties paid on millions of birds, it has recovered following legal protection and is now the most commonly seen large raptor across the Australian continent.

children.name: Aquila fasciata
children.description: A powerful medium-large eagle of rocky hillsides, cliffs, and forests from the Mediterranean to Southeast Asia, a bold hunter of partridges, hares, and other medium-sized prey. Vulnerable in parts of its European range due to persecution and disturbance at cliff nesting sites; populations in France and Iberia have been the focus of intensive conservation efforts.

children.name: Haliaeetus
children.description: Sea eagles — the largest eagles, specialising in fish, waterbirds, and carrion along coastlines, rivers, and lakes. The White-tailed Eagle is the largest eagle in Europe, with a wingspan exceeding 2.4 metres. It was exterminated from Britain by 1918 through poisoning, shooting, and egg collection, and has since been successfully reintroduced — first to Scotland in the 1970s, then to the Isle of Wight in 2019. The Bald Eagle — national emblem of the United States — recovered dramatically following DDT restrictions and delisting from the Endangered Species Act in 2007.

children.name: Haliaeetus albicilla
children.description: Europe's largest eagle, with a wingspan up to 2.4 m. Extinct in Britain by 1918; reintroduced to Scotland from Norway starting in 1975. Now around 150 pairs breed in Scotland, with birds expanding to Ireland and England.

children.name: Haliaeetus leucocephalus
children.description: The national bird of the United States, its white head and tail not acquired until the fourth or fifth year. DDT caused catastrophic eggshell thinning; after the ban in 1972 and intensive recovery programmes, it was delisted from the Endangered Species Act in 2007.

children.name: Haliaeetus pelagicus
children.description: One of the heaviest eagles in the world, with a massive orange-yellow bill, breeding on the coasts of far-eastern Russia and wintering in Japan. Vulnerable.

children.name: Haliaeetus vocifer
children.description: One of the most iconic birds of Africa, with a far-carrying, yodelling call — the quintessential sound of African waterways — that is recognised worldwide. The national bird of Zambia, Zimbabwe, Namibia, South Sudan, and Malawi; it plunges feet-first to snatch fish from the water surface in a dramatic aerial stoop.

children.name: Haliaeetus leucogaster
children.description: A large, pale-headed sea eagle of coasts, estuaries, rivers, and inland waters from India to Australia, conspicuous with its white head, neck, and underparts contrasting with dark grey upperparts. The second largest eagle in Australia, it is a versatile predator of fish, waterbirds, sea snakes, and carrion.

children.name: Haliaeetus humilis
children.description: A small fish-eating eagle of fast-flowing mountain rivers and forest streams in the Himalayas and Southeast Asia, replaced by the Grey-headed Fish Eagle in lowland habitats. Less well-known than its larger relatives, it is a dependable presence along clear, boulder-strewn mountain rivers from Nepal to Borneo.

children.name: Haliaeetus leucoryphus
children.description: A large, pale-headed sea eagle of Central Asian rivers, lakes, and wetlands, wintering south to India, Bangladesh, and Indochina. Vulnerable due to severe habitat loss and human disturbance; it nests in trees along major river systems but requires extensive undisturbed wetlands for foraging.

children.name: Haliaeetus vociferoides
children.description: A Critically Endangered sea eagle endemic to Madagascar, now largely restricted to coastal areas, estuaries, and large lakes in western and northern Madagascar. With fewer than 240 individuals estimated remaining, it is among the rarest raptors in the world, threatened by persecution, nest disturbance, and habitat degradation.

children.name: Circus
children.description: Harriers — slender, long-winged, long-tailed hawks that hunt by quartering low over marshland, moorland, and grassland on upswept wings. Their distinctive OWL-like facial disc gives them acute directional hearing for detecting prey in long vegetation. The Hen Harrier is one of the most contentious birds in Britain, where it is intensively persecuted on grouse moors and has been essentially eliminated as a breeding bird from England despite legal protection. Males are ghost-grey, females and immatures are streaky brown.

children.name: Circus cyaneus
children.description: A Red List species in Britain primarily due to illegal killing on driven grouse moors. Males are pale grey with black wing-tips; females are brown with a white rump. Displays spectacular sky-dancing courtship flights.

children.name: Circus aeruginosus
children.description: The largest European harrier, breeding in reedbeds and marshes. Reduced to near-extinction in Britain in the 20th century; now around 400 pairs following reedbed restoration and legal protection.

children.name: Circus buffoni
children.description: A large, long-winged harrier of South American grasslands, marshes, and wet pampas from Trinidad to Patagonia, the male striking black above with a pale grey rump and tail. Named after the French naturalist Georges-Louis Leclerc, Comte de Buffon; it is one of the largest harriers and hunts rodents and birds over extensive wetland and grassland.

children.name: Circus cinereus
children.description: A pale, grey harrier of Andean highland grasslands, puna, and Patagonian steppe from Colombia to Tierra del Fuego, the male ghostly silver-grey with finely barred underparts. It hunts rodents and small birds over open grasslands, nesting on the ground among dense tussock grass.

children.name: Circus melanoleucos
children.description: A striking black-and-white harrier of eastern Asian grasslands, wetlands, and rice paddies, breeding from Manchuria to Indochina and wintering to South Asia. The male is unmistakably patterned in bold black and white, making it one of the most distinctive members of the genus.

children.name: Circus macrourus
children.description: A pale, ghostly harrier of Eurasian grasslands and steppes that winters across South Asia and eastern Africa, the male strikingly white with minimal black wingtip markings. One of the rarest breeding harriers in Europe, Near Threatened due to degradation and conversion of steppe habitat.

children.name: Circus assimilis
children.description: A distinctive, richly spotted harrier of Australian grasslands, open woodland, and mallee scrub, heavily spotted below with rich chestnut. Unlike most harriers, which nest on the ground, the Spotted Harrier builds a stick nest in a tree — a unique habit within the genus.

children.name: Milvus
children.description: Red and Black kites — elegant, long-winged raptors with forked tails and a buoyant, effortless flight. The Red Kite is one of the great conservation success stories of recent decades: nearly exterminated in Britain (with only a small Welsh population surviving into the 20th century), it has been reintroduced to England, Scotland, and Ireland, and now numbers in the thousands. Medieval London was full of kites, which scavenged the streets — a historical parallel to the city kites of modern Nairobi.

children.name: Milvus milvus
children.description: One of Europe's most successful raptor reintroductions. British reintroduction in 1989 (Chilterns and Wales) now accounts for over 10% of the global population. The forked tail and rusty plumage are unmistakable in flight.

children.name: Milvus migrans
children.description: The most numerous raptor in the world. An adaptable scavenger of towns, rubbish dumps, rivers, and farmland. Millions funnel through the Strait of Gibraltar and Bosphorus on migration.

children.name: Elanus
children.description: Black-shouldered and black-winged kites — small, elegant kites with pearl-grey plumage and distinctive hovering hunting behaviour reminiscent of kestrels. They have expanded their ranges significantly in recent decades. The Black-winged Kite has colonised new parts of Europe as a breeding species, and the closely related Black-shouldered Kite is one of the most rapidly expanding raptors in Australia.

children.name: Elanus caeruleus
children.description: A small, pearl-grey kite with bold black shoulder patches and blood-red eyes, widespread from southern Europe and sub-Saharan Africa to South and Southeast Asia. It hunts rodents by hovering on rapidly beating wings before dropping onto prey, and has expanded its range significantly in Europe since the late 20th century.

children.name: Pernis
children.description: Honey buzzards — specialised wasp and hornet nest robbers. Despite the name they are more closely related to kites than true buzzards. The European Honey Buzzard spends much of its summer excavating bee and wasp nests from the ground, eating larvae, pupae, and comb — a diet almost unique among raptors. Scale-like feathers around the bill and eyes provide protection from stings. It is one of the latest spring migrants to arrive in Europe, timed to coincide with the establishment of wasp colonies.

children.name: Pernis apivorus
children.description: An enigmatic and variable raptor — plumage ranges from very pale to near-black. A strict long-distance migrant; most European birds winter in west Africa. Remarkably inconspicuous on the breeding ground despite open nest placement.

children.name: Aviceda
children.description: Bazas and cuckoo-hawks — crested, hawk-like raptors of Asian and Australasian forest with a distinctive owl-like facial expression and erect crest. They feed mainly on large insects, lizards, and frogs gleaned from the forest canopy, filling an ecological niche between hawks and large owls.

children.name: Aviceda leuphotes
children.description: A striking crested hawk of South and Southeast Asian forests, boldly patterned in black, white, and chestnut, with a prominent erect crest that gives it a dramatic appearance. It inhabits the canopy of tropical and subtropical broadleaf forest from northeast India to the Malay Peninsula and is a partial migrant, moving to lower elevations and south in winter.

children.name: Aviceda subcristata
children.description: A crested hawk of Australasian forest from the Moluccas to Queensland, closely related to the Black Baza. It feeds largely on tree frogs, lizards, and large insects gleaned from the forest canopy and is among the more colourful Australian raptors with its barred orange-and-white underparts.

children.name: Macheiramphus
children.description: The Bat Hawk — a slender, falcon-like accipitrid specialised for hunting bats and swiftlets at dusk and dawn, the only raptor to specialise in catching bats on the wing. It operates in near-darkness with proportionally very large eyes and catches prey in its gape rather than in its feet.

children.name: Macheiramphus alcinus
children.description: A slender, falcon-like raptor specialised for catching bats and swiftlets in flight at twilight, operating in near-darkness with very large eyes adapted for low light. It catches prey in its large gape rather than with its feet and swallows small bats whole in flight; found from sub-Saharan Africa to Southeast Asia near large bat roosts and cave systems.

children.name: Gyps
children.description: Griffon vultures — colonial, cliff-nesting carrion specialists of Africa, Europe, and Asia. Old World vultures evolved flight soaring independently from New World vultures (Cathartidae) and are more closely related to eagles and hawks. Griffon Vultures are the premier bone-cleaners of the Palearctic, descending in dozens on large carcasses and consuming them within hours. Their naked head and neck prevent feather fouling when reaching inside carcasses. Once common across much of Europe, reduced by poisoning and now recovering in southern Europe.

children.name: Gyps fulvus
children.description: Wingspan up to 2.8 m. Locates carcasses by sight and by watching the behaviour of other vultures. Soars on thermals to cover vast distances daily in search of food; may travel over 300 km from the roost to a carcass.

children.name: Gyps africanus
children.description: The most numerous large vulture in Africa and the most commonly seen raptor over African savanna, capable of stripping a zebra carcass within 20 minutes in a jostling mass of birds. Now Critically Endangered primarily due to poisoning — poachers lace carcasses with pesticides to kill vultures whose circling betrays the locations of illegally killed elephants.

children.name: Gyps bengalensis
children.description: Formerly one of the most abundant large birds in the world, this Asian vulture collapsed by over 99.9% in the 1990s due to diclofenac poisoning from treated livestock carcasses — one of the most sudden and catastrophic vertebrate population crashes in recorded history. Critically Endangered; recovery is dependent on banning veterinary diclofenac across South Asia.

children.name: Gyps indicus
children.description: A large, dark-backed vulture of peninsular India and Pakistan, also devastated by diclofenac poisoning from the 1990s onwards. Critically Endangered; it once formed vast roosts at Indian cities and played a central role in the Parsi sky burial tradition, which has collapsed in its absence.

children.name: Gyps himalayensis
children.description: The largest Old World vulture and one of the world's heaviest flying birds, with a wingspan reaching 3.1 m. Found on the Tibetan Plateau and high Himalayas up to 5,000 m, it is a significant participant in Tibetan sky burials and is one of the highest-soaring birds on Earth.

children.name: Gyps rueppelli
children.description: A large griffin of the African Sahel and East African mountains, named after the German naturalist Eduard Rüppell. Critically Endangered due to poisoning; this is the highest-flying bird ever reliably recorded, with one individual confirmed in a collision with a commercial aircraft at 11,300 m — well above the cruising altitude of most airliners.

children.name: Aegypius
children.description: The cinereous vulture, one of the largest birds of prey in the world, a massive Old World vulture of Eurasian mountains and steppes.

children.name: Aegypius monachus
children.description: The largest Old World vulture and one of the heaviest flying birds, soaring over the mountains and steppes of Eurasia. Near Threatened.

children.name: Neophron
children.description: The Egyptian vulture, a small migratory Old World vulture famous for using stones as tools to break open eggs.

children.name: Neophron percnopterus
children.description: A small migratory vulture famous for using stones as tools to crack open ostrich eggs. Endangered due to poisoning and persecution.

children.name: Gypaetus
children.description: The bearded vulture or lammergeier, a bone-eating mountain vulture of Eurasia and Africa, unique among raptors in its diet of bone marrow.

children.name: Gypaetus barbatus
children.description: The lammergeier, a spectacular mountain vulture that subsists largely on bone, dropping large bones onto rocks to shatter them. Near Threatened.

children.name: Circaetus
children.description: Snake eagles, raptors specialised in hunting reptiles, with broad heads and bare legs protective against snakebite.

children.name: Circaetus gallicus
children.description: A pale, large-headed eagle that hovers over open country hunting snakes and lizards, migrating from Europe to sub-Saharan Africa.

children.name: Circaetus pectoralis
children.description: A boldly patterned snake eagle of eastern and southern African savanna, with stark white underparts separated from dark upperparts by a solid black breast band. Like all snake eagles it has reinforced scales on the legs and feet as protection from biting prey, and swallows large snakes head-first in flight.

children.name: Circaetus cinereus
children.description: A large, uniformly brown snake eagle of sub-Saharan savanna woodlands, the most widespread African snake eagle. It hunts from exposed tree perches — sitting motionless for hours watching the ground for large snakes, monitor lizards, and other reptiles before stooping onto them.

children.name: Circaetus fasciolatus
children.description: A compact, banded snake eagle of coastal forest and dense woodland in eastern Africa from Somalia to KwaZulu-Natal, with fine brown barring on pale underparts and a single broad tail band. A forest specialist, less frequently encountered than the open-country snake eagles.

children.name: Spilornis
children.description: Serpent eagles — crested, large-eyed raptors of South and Southeast Asian forest specialised in hunting reptiles. They have broad heads with large yellow eyes and bare yellow ceres and facial skin, giving them an imposing, alert expression. The Crested Serpent Eagle is the most widespread member and among the most vocal raptors in Asian tropical forest.

children.name: Spilornis cheela
children.description: A widespread, charismatic eagle of South and Southeast Asian forest, with a prominent fan-like crest erected in display and striking bright yellow eyes. A specialist in snakes and lizards captured on the forest floor, it is one of the most vocal raptors in the region — its loud, piercing call is a characteristic sound of intact Asian tropical forest. With 23 subspecies spanning from India to the Lesser Sundas, it shows extraordinary geographic variation.

children.name: Clanga
children.description: Spotted eagles, medium-sized dark eagles of wetlands and forests in Eurasia, several of conservation concern.

children.name: Clanga pomarina
children.description: A forest eagle of eastern Europe that migrates in enormous flocks to southern Africa, hunting small mammals and amphibians in damp meadows.

children.name: Hieraaetus
children.description: Hawk-eagles — medium-sized, agile eagles of African and Asian forest and woodland, combining the power of a true eagle with the manoeuvrability of a large Accipiter. Several species were long placed in Aquila and show intermediate characteristics between the two genera.

children.name: Hieraaetus wahlbergi
children.description: A slender, all-dark eagle of sub-Saharan woodland and savanna, with a characteristic small crest visible at close range and a distinctive long, square-tipped tail. Named after the Swedish naturalist Johan August Wahlberg; it is a long-distance intra-African migrant and one of the more commonly observed medium eagles in southern Africa.

children.name: Hieraaetus spilogaster
children.description: A powerful, boldly marked eagle of sub-Saharan forest and dense woodland, the underparts white with bold black streaking visible from long range. A versatile and aggressive predator of monkeys, guineafowl, francolins, and other medium-sized prey, it is widespread across well-wooded Africa and among the more formidable resident eagles.

children.name: Hieraaetus kienerii
children.description: A compact, distinctive forest eagle of the Himalayas and Southeast Asia, with a blackish hood, white throat, and rich rufous underparts. It inhabits dense broadleaf forest at moderate elevations from Pakistan to the Philippines, hunting birds and lizards by rapid stoops from perches in the forest canopy.

children.name: Terathopius
children.description: The Bateleur — a unique, short-tailed eagle of African savanna whose dramatic acrobatic flight earned it the French name 'bateleur' (street performer or juggler). It is unmistakeable in flight — the extremely short tail, broad wings, bright red face and feet, and contrasting black, white, and chestnut plumage make it one of the most visually striking raptors in the world.

children.name: Terathopius ecaudatus
children.description: A spectacular short-tailed eagle with a fast, rocking, acrobatic flight style — the bird constantly tips from side to side, like a tightrope walker balancing — over vast stretches of African savanna. Covers hundreds of kilometres daily at speeds up to 80 km/h, scavenging and pirating kills from other raptors. Near Threatened due to persecution, habitat loss, and poisoning.

children.name: Lophaetus
children.description: The Long-crested Eagle — a small, distinctive African eagle with a remarkably long, floppy crest that droops forward in a characteristically dishevelled manner. It favours moist grassland, cultivation, and forest edges near water, where it specialises in catching rodents.

children.name: Lophaetus occipitalis
children.description: A small, compact African eagle with a distinctively long, floppy crest and bold white wing patches that flash conspicuously in its buoyant, undulating flight. Common in moist woodland and cultivation margins from West Africa to South Africa, it specialises almost exclusively on rodents and is attracted to recently cut grassland and roadsides where prey is exposed.

children.name: Stephanoaetus
children.description: The African Crowned Eagle — widely considered the most powerful eagle in Africa relative to body size, and the apex avian predator of the African forest. It kills prey several times its own body weight using its huge, crushing talons.

children.name: Stephanoaetus coronatus
children.description: Widely considered the most powerful eagle in Africa relative to body size, capable of killing prey several times its own weight including small antelopes, monkeys, and large monitor lizards. It hunts by explosive ambush from concealment — a contrasting style to the soaring eagles — and its nest is a massive stick platform used and added to for many decades.

children.name: Polemaetus
children.description: The Martial Eagle — the largest eagle in Africa, an apex predator of savanna and open woodland. Its wingspan and grip strength are among the greatest of any raptor on the African continent.

children.name: Polemaetus bellicosus
children.description: The largest eagle in Africa, with a wingspan approaching 1.9 m and crushing grip documented to be capable of breaking bones. Vulnerable; it requires vast, intact territories and has declined markedly as savanna is converted to agriculture. It is persecuted by farmers who attribute livestock losses to it — often incorrectly — and its low reproductive rate makes population recovery extremely slow.
